text: 197 minutes, 11,820 seconds, 3 hours, and 17 minutes. That is the amount of time the Avatar, Fire and Ash drained out of my personal hourglass, never to be returned. >> I, immediately, regret, this, decision. >> So,, how, best, can, I, describe, the experience of this movie? Well, imagine going on a date with a really hot girl or guy for the other 6% of my audience only to discover they've got absolutely no personality and nothing even remotely interesting to say. The first hour

passes tolerably enough because if nothing else, they're pleasant enough to look at and you're able to coast along with idol small talk while you get quietly drunk. By the second hour you're starting to get bored and distracted and kind of regretting your decision to come along for this thing. And by the third hour, you're just desperate for it to end. and seriously considering slipping off to the bathroom, calling an Uber, and getting the hell out of there. Fire and ass is

practically a test of human endurance and determination. A bloated, cliched overroought melodramatic pretentious vanity project by a once great filmmaker who hasn't heard the word no in almost 30 years. A man whose fame and success has given him all the time and money in the world to indulge his worst excesses. And believe me, he does. Just like that date you wish you hadn't gone on, it definitely looks fantastic, and I can't fault the sheer money and effort that went into its visuals. But it's

ultimately a hollow and meaningless experience, going nowhere with a bunch of characters you don't particularly care about, taking forever to do it, and ultimately ending up right back where it started. A walking shadow, a poor player that struts and frets his hour upon the stage, and then is heard no more. A tale told by an idiot, full of sound and fury, signifying nothing. >> Just, consume, product, and, then, get excited for next product. >> Jesus, Christ., Let's, just, get, this, [ __ ]

show underway so I can do something more worthwhile, like cutting the brake cables on my neighbor's car. Yeah, I'll give you boundary dispute you prick. Anyway, fire and ass picks up shortly after the events of, I don't know, whatever the last movie was called. Everyone's kind of sad now because one of generic protagonists sons got killed. And honestly, it wasn't until this movie specifically reminded me about it that I even remembered he'd existed. Anyway, there's bigger problems

to deal with in the shape of Spider their adopted human son, who obviously can't breathe the air on Pandora and almost dies when his face mask runs out of batteries. Generic protagonist decides that he can't stay with them anymore. It only took him like 18 years to realize this. So, they hitch a ride with a bunch of traveling sky traders or whatever the hell they're supposed to be to take him back to the humans. But oh no, they get attacked by evil fire people led by evil fire lady. Why

exactly are they evil? Why are they attacking their own people when there's an existential threat to the whole planet to deal with? Don't know. They just are. So, the ship crashes and everyone gets separated in the chaos and Spider dies when his mask runs out of air. Wow. It's funny how that was totally never an issue in the previous movie, but now that it's a plot point the [ __ ] thing needs to be swapped out like every 20 minutes. Anyway, it turns out to be fine, though, because

creepy teenage Sigourney Weaver, who's actually the key to everything downloads a firmware update into him that allows him to breathe the air. Now how the [ __ ] does that even begin to work? Don't know. >> Don't, ask, questions. >> What, I, do, know, is, that, Colonel, Quaric shows up. Well, that was lucky, wasn't it? And naturally, he's pretty interested in Spider because if they can duplicate what's been done to him, then humans can survive on Pandora without masks.

Wait, hold up. Let's just stop this for a second. I'm sorry to go off on a tangent here, but can someone please explain to me what exactly the human objectives are on Pandora? Because by George, they seem to change more frequently than my [ __ ] underwear are. Like in the first movie, it was basically just a mining operation to grab the magical [ __ ] mineral on the planet. And the soldiers were there just as hired guns to protect the operation. Then in the second movie, it was all

about sucking out the brain goop from the space whales to make people live forever or something. Now they just want to fullon colonize the entire planet. Why? I mean, I'm no expert on planetary colonization, but speaking as a layman here, if I was looking for a planet to settle on, I'm pretty sure my first priority would be a breathable atmosphere. Like, are you seriously telling me that Pandora is literally the only planet in the galaxy that humanity can possibly colonize? But, okay, fine.

Just for the sake of argument, let's say that this literally is mankind's only option for long-term survival. If so how exactly does that make them evil? I mean, they're just trying to stay alive like any living species. If the Na'vi faced the same dilemma and they actually had the technology for interstellar travel, they'd almost certainly do the same thing because ultimately their own survival would take priority over any species that stood in their way. In fact, I'd argue that expansion and

conquest isn't just a necessary step for survival. It's an absolute biological imperative. Humanity's manifest destiny isn't just to reach for the stars, but to seize them in an iron grip and never let go. And that means taking the fight to our enemies, wherever they may be. The demon, the heretic, the xenos, all shall be purged in the holy fires of war and conquest. All shall fall before the unstoppable might of our armies until the entire galaxy knows only one thing.

the eternal grip of the Imperium of Man. Sorry, lost my train of thought there. What the hell was I talking about? Oh yeah, the Na'vi. So, let's go with the assumption that humanity absolutely does need to colonize this particular planet. Well, that would leave them with only one viable course of action, the complete annihilation of the Na'vi as a rival species. Because, let's be honest they're clearly hostile, and every time humans have gone up against them in

battle, they've lost. They represent a clear and present danger to human survival. So why [ __ ] around with all these little peacemeal attacks? In fact why even bother fighting them at all when there are other more viable options? For example, an airborne pathogen specifically designed to kill that one particular species would solve the entire problem within a matter of months without the need for costly battles or damage to Pandora's ecosystem. [ __ ] man. The common flu

would probably be deadly for them since they'd have no natural immunity to human diseases. And if we really want to stick with the Native American allegory, which we clearly do with these movies, that would actually be pretty close to reality. It was diseases like smallox that really defeated them, not a bunch of settlers with flint lock musketss. Anyway, those are questions for other smarter movies. Let's just get back to this one. So, Quaric naturally wants

Spider, but evil Fire Lady wants Quaric because he has access to guns, and she likes guns because they go boom. A woman after my own heart. Now, considering the aforementioned allegory for Native American colonization, I guess this is the film's attempt to reluctantly acknowledge that Native Americans weren't exactly a peaceful, harmonious utopia before the dreaded white boys got there. Anyway, the upshot is that Quaric and the humans are now alloyed with the evil fire people. So, Generic

protagonist and his family are under threat from all sides. So, there's a bunch of chases and a bunch of battles and a bunch of different characters get taken prisoner, but nobody ever dies because this is basically a soap opera with a AAA budget. And generic protagonist delivers a rousing speech to rally all the clans around him. And it's just in time for, I don't know, a big final battle that's the same exact setup as the other two final battles from the

other movies. And just when it all seems lost, a DXX Pandora conveniently steps in to save the day with a bunch of animals that suddenly decide they're capable of understanding orders and forming battle plans. And the humans get defeated again. And the Na'vi celebrate again. And there's some earnest speeches about, I don't know, the importance of family and sticking together. And

Oh, [ __ ] sake. The short version of all this [ __ ] is that if you've seen the first and second Avatar movies, you've basically seen this one, too. The formula is the same. It's just the values that are bigger this time around. And the result well, it's a long movie. In fact, just like Tatiana, I think it's about time we talked about that excessive length. At well north of three hours and 15 minutes. Fire and ass is abusively long. Especially when you realize the plot

could be easily condensed into like half that, time., I'm, not, kidding., At least, 50% of this movie is nothing but a wanky tech demo. Just endless landscape and wildlife shots that go on forever and accomplish absolutely nothing. A flamboyant $400 million screens saver that adds nothing to the story or characters and bogs down what's already a frustrating and repetitive narrative. I kid you not. Here, characters get captured and taken hostage and have to be rescued on like four different

occasions. It just keeps recycling the same [ __ ] beats over and over, and there's no tension or drama because, you know, that no one important is ever going to get killed in these things. You know, for a franchise that Cameron describes as a passion project, I can't say that I detected a whole lot of passion or imagination on the writing side of things. Like, what happened to the aliens and Terminator 2 energy? What happened to the James Cameron we knew from the 80s and 90s?

>> Did, IQ's, just, drop, sharply, while, I, was away? >> Yeah,, the, guy, definitely, cares, about film making technology, even if he's like the only man on earth who still has a hard on for 3D. But I can't shake the feeling that maybe he's getting a bit tired of the whole Avatar thing now. Quote, I've got other stories to tell and I've got other stories to tell within Avatar. What won't happen is I won't go down the rabbit hole of exclusively making only Avatar for

multiple years. I'm going to figure out a way that involves more collaboration. I'm, not, saying, I'm, going to, step, away, as a director, but I'm going to pull back from being as hands-on with every tiny aspect of the process. Hm. Maybe he's beginning to realize that 25 years is a hell of a long time to invest in three movies, especially when you're 70 years old. Like I say, visually it looks fantastic and all that, but it does suffer from the same problem you always

get with CGI. There's basically no weight or impact to anything that happens because, you know, it was all just rendered on a computer. Also, the scenes with Spider do kind of make me laugh. One, because the actor's so [ __ ] wooden, you can make a log cabin out of him. And two, because he's the only physically real thing on screen it's pretty obvious when everything else around him is fake. As for the other characters, they're the usual onenote walking cliches you'd expect from these

movies. Generic protagonist is still just a generic good guy trying to hold his family together and do the right thing. Evil fire lady is evil and likes fire because the movie needed another antagonist. I guess the kids are all a bunch of nothing burgers to the point where I struggle to even remember who was who. Creepy teenage Sigourney Weaver is still creepy as [ __ ] because she's got the body of a young girl but the voice of an old woman. And I don't care

how much you try to make her the key to everything. I'll never understand why this character is in the [ __ ] movie. The only character with even a grain of personality is Quaric. And I suspect that's mostly down to a strong performance by Steven Lang. Generally though, if you asked me to describe the personalities of any specific character in this movie, I would totally struggle because there's nothing to describe. In terms of themes, the anti-colonial anti- capitalist, anti-military

messaging is all present and correct. And honestly, there's not much I can add to it that I haven't said in my previous reviews. I will say though that it's become particularly on the nose this time around. Here's a fun little drinking game you can play at home kids. Have a careful look at the human characters in Avatar. the brutal soldiers, the cruel whale hunters, the evil corporate types, all the people you're supposed to hate, and take a shot every time you spot a non-white actor on

screen, even in the backgrounds. I can pretty much guarantee you'll be stone cold sober by the end of the movie. Why? Because there's none to be found here. And it's strange because normally you can't move for the on-screen diversity in Disney movies, which are determined to reflect the world we live in today. I wonder why they dropped the ball so suddenly with this particular film. I wonder why they chose to have this violent destructive expansionist capitalist, militaristic dictatorship

represented almost entirely by one ethnic group. >> Well,, I, couldn't, possibly, solve, this mystery. Can you? >> As, a, great, man, once, said, a, long, time ago, you might not have noticed that but your brain did. So, how the hell do I sum up my thoughts on the Avatar films? Well, remember the Star Wars prequels? Remember how most people generally agree that the biggest problem with those movies was George Lucas himself and his complete inability to get out of his own way? Remember how

most people conceded that he'd become too rich and too powerful and there was nobody left around to call him out on his [ __ ] and re in his worst impulses as a filmmaker? Well, I think James Cameron seems to have walked the same path. Because when you get right down to it, that's what the Avatar movies are. proof that all the money and all the visuals and all the resources in the world are no substitute for a decent [ __ ] script. Will people still flock

to, see, it, no matter, what, I, say, here? Sure. Will it still make a [ __ ] ton of money? Almost certainly, but probably less than the last movie, which also made less than the first. Will anyone even remember it a week after it leaves cinemas? Probably not. But such is the way of Avatar. It's the ultimate hollow victory. A pile of money you can't spend. A piece of art with no meaning. A story with no purpose. A film that absolutely isn't worth 197 minutes of your life.
Anyway, that's all I've got for today. Go away now.
